questions.stem: The two arms joined at the centromere on the right-hand chromosome are genetically identical copies. During the normal cell cycle they are generated by which event?

explanation.summary: Sister chromatids are the two identical copies of a chromosome produced by DNA replication in S phase; they remain joined at the centromere until separated in anaphase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sister chromatids are the two identical copies of a chromosome produced by DNA replication in S phase; they remain joined at the centromere until separated in anaphase.

questions.stem: The complete division cycle diagrammed here yields daughter cells with which chromosomal content relative to the original parent cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two diploid cells genetically identical to the parent

choices.label: C
choices.text: Two haploid cells identical to the parent

explanation.summary: Mitosis produces two daughter cells that are diploid (2n) and genetically identical to the parent, in contrast to meiosis which yields four genetically unique haploid cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitosis produces two daughter cells that are diploid (2n) and genetically identical to the parent, in contrast to meiosis which yields four genetically unique haploid cells.

questions.stem: The two-stage division sequence shown, which includes pairing of homologous chromosomes and crossing over, ultimately produces which final products?

choices.label: C
choices.text: One diploid and one haploid cell

explanation.summary: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (reductional) and meiosis II separates sister chromatids, yielding four haploid cells that are genetically unique due to crossing over and independent assortment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (reductional) and meiosis II separates sister chromatids, yielding four haploid cells that are genetically unique due to crossing over and independent assortment.

questions.stem: This laboratory study displays an individual's chromosomes arranged in numbered pairs plus the sex chromosomes. How many total chromosomes are present in this normal set?

explanation.summary: A normal human somatic karyotype contains 46 chromosomes: 22 autosome pairs plus two sex chromosomes (XY shown here). Deviations such as 45 or 47 indicate aneuploidy (e.g., monosomy or trisomy).
explanation.whyCorrect: A normal human somatic karyotype contains 46 chromosomes: 22 autosome pairs plus two sex chromosomes (XY shown here). Deviations such as 45 or 47 indicate aneuploidy (e.g., monosomy or trisomy).

questions.stem: What cytogenetic laboratory technique produced this image of chromosomes sorted and arranged by size and banding pattern?

explanation.summary: Karyotyping arrests dividing cells in metaphase, stains the condensed chromosomes, and arranges them by size, centromere position, and banding pattern to detect numerical and large structural chromosomal abnormalities.
explanation.whyCorrect: Karyotyping arrests dividing cells in metaphase, stains the condensed chromosomes, and arranges them by size, centromere position, and banding pattern to detect numerical and large structural chromosomal abnormalities.

questions.stem: Based on the number and identity of the chromosomes shown, what does this karyotype represent clinically?

explanation.summary: Twenty-two autosome pairs plus one X and one Y, with no numerical abnormality, defines a normal euploid male. Klinefelter (XXY), Turner (45,X), and Down syndrome would each show an abnormal count.
explanation.whyCorrect: Twenty-two autosome pairs plus one X and one Y, with no numerical abnormality, defines a normal euploid male. Klinefelter (XXY), Turner (45,X), and Down syndrome would each show an abnormal count.

questions.stem: The highlighted group of chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22) whose centromeres sit very near one end are clinically emphasized because they characteristically participate in which event?

explanation.summary: The acrocentric chromosomes have centromeres near one end with small short arms, allowing their long arms to fuse at the centromere in Robertsonian translocations, an important cause of translocation Down syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: The acrocentric chromosomes have centromeres near one end with small short arms, allowing their long arms to fuse at the centromere in Robertsonian translocations, an important cause of translocation Down syndrome.

questions.stem: The arrow points to three copies of chromosome 21 in this karyotype. What clinical syndrome does this abnormality produce?

explanation.summary: Three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21) cause Down syndrome, characterized by intellectual disability, characteristic facies, and increased risk of congenital heart defects. Edwards (18) and Patau (13) are other autosomal trisomies.
explanation.whyCorrect: Three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21) cause Down syndrome, characterized by intellectual disability, characteristic facies, and increased risk of congenital heart defects. Edwards (18) and Patau (13) are other autosomal trisomies.

questions.stem: A phenotypically normal person is found to be a balanced carrier of the chromosomal rearrangement fusing chromosomes 14 and 21 shown here. This carrier is at increased risk of having a child with which condition?

explanation.summary: A rob(14;21) Robertsonian translocation carrier is balanced and healthy, but during gamete formation can produce unbalanced gametes carrying the extra chromosome 21 material, leading to translocation Down syndrome in offspring.
explanation.whyCorrect: A rob(14;21) Robertsonian translocation carrier is balanced and healthy, but during gamete formation can produce unbalanced gametes carrying the extra chromosome 21 material, leading to translocation Down syndrome in offspring.

questions.stem: The upper structural chromosome abnormality depicted (loss of the terminal short arm of chromosome 5) produces which classic clinical syndrome?

explanation.summary: Terminal deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-) causes cri-du-chat syndrome, characterized by a high-pitched cat-like cry, microcephaly, and intellectual disability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Terminal deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-) causes cri-du-chat syndrome, characterized by a high-pitched cat-like cry, microcephaly, and intellectual disability.

questions.stem: This karyotype shows an extra copy of chromosome 21 (circled). What meiotic error most commonly gives rise to this finding?

explanation.summary: Most cases of trisomy 21 result from meiotic nondisjunction, the failure of homologous chromosomes (or sister chromatids) to separate, producing a gamete with an extra chromosome 21. Risk rises with advanced maternal age.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most cases of trisomy 21 result from meiotic nondisjunction, the failure of homologous chromosomes (or sister chromatids) to separate, producing a gamete with an extra chromosome 21. Risk rises with advanced maternal age.

questions.stem: This diagram shows chromosomes 4 and 20 exchanging segments. This type of rearrangement between two non-homologous chromosomes is specifically called a:

explanation.summary: A reciprocal translocation is a mutual exchange of segments between two non-homologous chromosomes. When no genetic material is net gained or lost the carrier is usually phenotypically normal, but their gametes may be unbalanced.
explanation.whyCorrect: A reciprocal translocation is a mutual exchange of segments between two non-homologous chromosomes. When no genetic material is net gained or lost the carrier is usually phenotypically normal, but their gametes may be unbalanced.

questions.stem: A person carries a balanced reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 12 and 14 with no net loss of genetic material. What is the main clinical risk this poses?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Producing gametes with unbalanced chromosome content, risking miscarriage or an affected child

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spontaneous development of trisomy 21 in the carrier

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediate development of leukemia in the carrier

explanation.summary: Balanced translocation carriers are typically phenotypically normal because total genetic material is preserved. During meiosis, however, segregation can yield gametes with duplicated or deleted segments (unbalanced), increasing the risk of recurrent pregnancy loss or a child with a chromosomal disorder.
explanation.whyCorrect: Balanced translocation carriers are typically phenotypically normal because total genetic material is preserved. During meiosis, however, segregation can yield gametes with duplicated or deleted segments (unbalanced), increasing the risk of recurrent pregnancy loss or a child with a chromosomal disorder.

questions.stem: The structural chromosome abnormality shown on the right-hand chromosome results in which change to the cell's genetic dosage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gain of genetic material (partial trisomy of the affected segment)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exchange of material between homologous chromosomes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reversal of gene order with no dosage change

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss of genetic material for the affected segment

explanation.summary: A duplication adds an extra copy of a chromosomal segment, increasing gene dosage for that region and producing a partial trisomy, unlike deletions (loss) or inversions (no net dosage change).
explanation.whyCorrect: A duplication adds an extra copy of a chromosomal segment, increasing gene dosage for that region and producing a partial trisomy, unlike deletions (loss) or inversions (no net dosage change).

questions.stem: In the process shown, a chromosomal segment that does NOT include the centromere is excised, flipped, and reinserted. Which structural abnormality does this represent?

explanation.summary: An inversion in which the reversed segment lies entirely on one arm and excludes the centromere is a paracentric inversion; a pericentric inversion would span the centromere.
explanation.whyCorrect: An inversion in which the reversed segment lies entirely on one arm and excludes the centromere is a paracentric inversion; a pericentric inversion would span the centromere.
